title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text: In this lab, I applied spatial predictive modeling techniques using the 311 service request type ‘Alley Lights Out’ violation as the predictor variable. I first cleaned and mapped the data to visualize the spatial distribution of reported outages and identify clusters. Using exploratory spatial analysis, I detected hotspot areas where complaints were concentrated. I then built a predictive model using location-based features to estimate the likelihood of future alley light outages across different areas. The model results showed that outages tend to cluster in specific regions, suggesting underlying spatial patterns such as infrastructure conditions or neighborhood characteristics. Overall, this analysis demonstrates how spatial modeling can help predict service needs and support more efficient allocation of city maintenance resources.

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
My Objectives

In this assignment, my objective is to apply the spatial predictive modeling techniques learned in class to a new 311 service request type, specifically Alley Lights Out. I aim to adapt the provided example code to analyze this dataset, create meaningful spatial features, and use appropriate count regression models to examine burglary incidents as the outcome variable, while incorporating Alley Lights Out as a key spatial predictor along with other spatial features. Additionally, I seek to implement spatial cross-validation to evaluate model performance, interpret the results clearly, and critically assess how well the model explains the spatial distribution of burglary across Chicago.



“I have selected the 311 service request type ‘Alley Lights Out’ as Violation type for this analysis becasue it interests me. This violation type focuses on reports of non-functioning alley lighting, which is important for public safety and urban infrastructure maintenance. It differs from the abandoned cars example covered in class and provides a meaningful basis for exploring spatial patterns and building a predictive model.


Total Number of calls = 218912


Data Loading & Exploration

The required datasets have been successfully loaded, including the 311 service request data for ‘Alley Lights Out’, as well as the spatial boundary layers for Chicago city limits, police districts, and police beats. These datasets will be used to explore spatial patterns and support further analysis. burglaries data has also been loaded here.


Police districts: 25

Police beats: 277

Number of burglaries: 7482

CRS: ESRI:102271

The spatial distribution of the ‘police districts’ violation type has been created and is shown below.









The spatial distribution of the ‘police Beats’ violation type has been created and is shown below.









The outline/boundary of the Chicago has been created and is shown below.









The spatial distribution of the ‘Alley Lights Out’ violation type has been created and is shown below.











Patterns observed

The spatial distribution of “Alley Lights Out” 311 requests is not evenly spread across the city but instead shows clear clustering. There are many reports concentrated in the central, western, and southern parts of the city, where points are very dense, indicating frequent complaints about alley light outages. In contrast, the northern areas have fewer reports, suggesting either fewer outages or differences in reporting behavior. Overall, the points form noticeable clusters rather than being randomly scattered, which means nearby locations tend to experience similar issues. At the same time, these requests are present across most parts of the city, showing that the problem exists citywide. This clustering pattern suggests that alley light outages are influenced by local conditions and could be useful for spatial analysis and predicting related outcomes like crime.



Fishnet Grid

A 500m x 500m fishnet grid has been successfully created for the study area. The grid consists of 2,458 cells, with each cell measuring 500 x 500 meters and covering an area of 250,000 square meters. These grid cells will be used as the spatial units for further analysis and modeling. Wee only Kept cells that intersect within Chicago bondary.



















Aggregation of Alley Lights Out Violations to Fishnet Grid:

The selected 311 violation type, ‘Alley Lights Out’, has been successfully aggregated to the fishnet grid by counting the number of violations within each cell. Out of the total 2,458 grid cells, 362 cells (14.7%) contain zero Alley Lights Out violations, while 2,096 cells have at least one recorded violation, indicating that most areas experienced some level of reported outages.”



Aggregation of Burglary Incidents to Fishnet Grid

The burglary incidents have also been aggregated to the same fishnet grid by counting the number of burglaries within each cell (countBurglaries). Out of the total 2,458 grid cells, 781 cells (31.8%) contain zero burglary incidents, while 1,677 cells have at least one recorded burglary. Compared to the Alley Lights Out violations (which had only 14.7% zero cells), burglary incidents are more sparsely distributed across the study area.



Visualize the count distribution of ‘Alley Lights Out’

This figure shows the spatial distribution of “Alley Lights Out” complaints across Chicago using a 500m × 500m fishnet grid. Each grid cell is colored based on the number of reported complaints, with lighter (yellow) areas indicating higher counts and darker (purple) areas indicating fewer or no reports. The map reveals clear spatial clustering, where certain neighborhoods—particularly in parts of the central and southern areas—experience higher concentrations of outage complaints. This suggests that alley light issues are not randomly distributed, but instead complaints are reported more frequently in specific locations, possibly due to infrastructure conditions or neighborhood characteristics.












Visualize the count distribution of Burglary

This figure shows the spatial distribution of burglary incidents across Chicago using a 500m × 500m fishnet grid. Each grid cell is shaded based on the number of reported burglaries, with lighter (yellow) areas indicating higher counts and darker (purple) areas indicating fewer or no incidents. The map highlights clear spatial clustering of burglary incidents, with certain areas—particularly parts of the central and southern regions—experiencing higher concentrations of burglaries. This pattern suggests that burglary is not randomly distributed across the city, but is instead more prevalent in specific locations, potentially influenced by factors such as neighborhood characteristics, land use, or socio-economic conditions.

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
k-nearest neighbors

In this step, spatial features were generated by calculating k-nearest neighbor distances to capture the local context of incidents. In addition to counting the number of events within each grid cell, the distance to the nearest three neighboring incidents was computed to better understand spatial relationships. The results show that the nearest neighbor distances vary considerably across the study area, with a minimum distance of 2.711 meters, a median of 68.830 meters, and a mean distance of 169.671 meters, indicating moderate clustering in some regions and wider spacing in others. The maximum distance of 1706.739 meters suggests that some cells are relatively isolated, highlighting spatial variability in the distribution of incidents.


Local Moran’s I analysis

Let’s identify clusters of Alley Lights Out using Local Moran’s I, then calculate distance to these hot spots. First identification of hot spots and cold spots











This figure presents the Local Moran’s I analysis for “Alley Lights Out” complaints, identifying spatial clusters across Chicago. The map highlights statistically significant clusters, where red (High–High) cells represent areas with high numbers of alley light outage complaints surrounded by neighboring cells that also have high complaint counts—these are considered hotspots of outage complaints. A small number of light blue (Low–High) cells indicate areas with low complaints surrounded by higher-count neighbors, suggesting spatial outliers. Most of the city appears in gray (not significant), meaning there is no strong spatial clustering in those locations. Overall, the map shows that outage complaints are spatially concentrated in specific areas rather than randomly distributed, with notable hotspots particularly in parts of the southern region. This supports the presence of spatial dependence and justifies the use of spatial features in the predictive modeling.



Distance-to-hotspot measures

The results indicate that 235 grid cells have been identified as hotspot areas for Alley Lights Out complaints. This means there are a substantial number of locations where high levels of complaints are spatially clustered together. The presence of these hotspots confirms that outage complaints are not randomly distributed, but instead concentrated in specific parts of the city. Additionally, calculating the distance from each grid cell to the nearest hotspot provides an important spatial measure of proximity, showing how close or far different areas are from these high-complaint zones. Overall, this result highlights the existence of localized problem areas and supports the use of spatial features in predicting and understanding patterns of alley light outage complaints.

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
Joinned Police Districts for Cross-Validation
We are using police districts for our spatial cross-validation.

This step integrates police district boundaries with the fishnet grid to support spatial cross-validation. As a result, 22 police districts are included in the analysis, and 1,708 grid cells fall within these districts. This ensures that only relevant spatial units within official administrative boundaries are used for modeling. The outcome is important because it allows the analysis to be structured around real-world geographic divisions, improving the reliability of model evaluation and making the results more meaningful for policy and resource allocation decisions.

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
Check for Overdispersion
Poisson regression assumes mean = variance. Real count data often violates this (over dispersion).

Results
Dispersion parameter: 3.07
Rule of thumb: >1.5 suggests overdispersion
Overdispersion detected! Consider Negative Binomial model.


Now we will use Fit Negative Binomial regression
(Dispersion parameter for Negative Binomial(1.7868)
Model Comparison:
Poisson AIC: 8788.4
Negative Binomial AIC: 7401.9

The Poisson regression model initially showed a dispersion parameter of 3.07, which is greater than the rule-of-thumb threshold of 1.5, indicating overdispersion in the data. This means that the variability in burglary counts is higher than what the Poisson model assumes, making it less appropriate for this analysis. To address this issue, a Negative Binomial model was fitted, which is better suited for overdispersed count data. Comparing the two models using AIC (where lower values indicate better fit), the Negative Binomial model (AIC = 7401.9) performs substantially better than the Poisson model (AIC = 8788.4). This comparison suggests that the Negative Binomial model provides a more accurate and reliable representation of the burglary data, and is therefore the preferred model for this analysis.



Spatial Cross-Validation (2017)
Implement Leave-One-Group-Out cross-validation on 2017 data
Mean MAE: 2.47
Mean RMSE: 3.4

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
Generate Final Predictions










This figure shows the Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) baseline for burglary incidents in Chicago, which represents a smoothed surface of burglary locations. Instead of showing exact counts per grid cell, the KDE map highlights the intensity or concentration of burglary incidents across space. Areas shown in lighter colors (yellow) indicate locations with a higher density of burglaries, while darker areas (purple) represent lower density. The map reveals clear hotspot regions, particularly in parts of the central and southern areas, where burglaries are more concentrated. This visualization serves as a baseline model, helping to identify general spatial patterns of crime without incorporating additional predictors, and provides a useful comparison for evaluating the performance of more advanced predictive models.

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
Model Evaluation
Comparison to KDE baseline










This figure compares actual burglary counts, predictions from the Negative Binomial model, and the KDE baseline predictions to evaluate model performance. The first map shows the observed distribution of burglaries across grid cells, while the second map presents the model’s predicted counts using spatial features such as Alley Lights Out and hotspot distances. The third map shows predictions from the simpler KDE approach, which only captures general spatial intensity.
Visually, the Negative Binomial model predictions more closely resemble the actual burglary pattern, capturing finer spatial variations and localized hotspots. In contrast, the KDE baseline appears more smoothed and generalized, missing some of the detailed variations seen in the actual data. This comparison indicates that the statistical model provides a more accurate and nuanced prediction of burglary patterns than the simple KDE approach, demonstrating the value of incorporating explanatory spatial features in predictive modeling.




Model Performance Comparison


model_mae
model_rmse
kde_mae
kde_rmse

The picture compares two maps showing how accurate a burglary prediction model is across different areas. The first map shows whether the model predicted too many or too few burglaries. Blue regions mean the model predicted more burglaries than actually happened, while red regions mean it predicted fewer burglaries than occurred. White areas show places where the prediction was close to correct. The second map focuses only on how large the prediction mistakes are. Dark colors represent small errors, while brighter colors represent larger errors. Together, the two maps show both the direction and the size of the model’s mistakes, helping identify which areas are predicted well and which areas need improvement.

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
You are a data analyst for the [Pennsylvania] Department of Human Services. The department is considering implementing an algorithmic system to identify communities that should receive priority for social service funding and outreach programs. Your supervisor has asked you to evaluate the quality and reliability of available census data to inform this decision.
Drawing on our Week 2 discussion of algorithmic bias, you need to assess not just what the data shows, but how reliable it is and what communities might be affected by data quality issues.




Apply dplyr functions to real census data for policy analysis
Evaluate data quality using margins of error
Connect technical analysis to algorithmic decision-making
Identify potential equity implications of data reliability issues
Create professional documentation for policy stakeholders

Make sure to update your _quarto.yml navigation to include this assignment under an “Labs” menu.

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
2.2 Data Quality Assessment
Your Task: Calculate margin of error percentages and create reliability categories.
Requirements: - Calculate MOE percentage: (margin of error / estimate) * 100 - Create reliability categories: - High Confidence: MOE < 5% - Moderate Confidence: MOE 5-10%
- Low Confidence: MOE > 10% - Create a flag for unreliable estimates (MOE > 10%)
Hint: Use mutate() with case_when() for the categories.

# Calculate MOE percentage and reliability categories using mutate()

# Create a summary showing count of counties in each reliability category
# Hint: use count() and mutate() to add percentages

MOE <- pa_clean %>%
  mutate(
    # Calculate MOE as percentage of estimate
    moe_percentage = round((median_household_incomeM / median_household_incomeE) * 100, 2),
    
    # Create reliability categories
    reliability = case_when(
      moe_percentage < 5 ~ "High Confidence",
      moe_percentage >= 5 & moe_percentage <= 10 ~ "Moderate",
      moe_percentage > 10 ~ "Low Confidence"
    )
  )

count(MOE, reliability)

# A tibble: 2 × 2
  reliability         n
  <chr>           <int>
1 High Confidence    57
2 Moderate           10

View(MOE)

text:
3.2 Tract-Level Demographics
Your Task: Get demographic data for census tracts in your selected counties.
Requirements: - Geography: tract level - Variables: white alone (B03002_003), Black/African American (B03002_004), Hispanic/Latino (B03002_012), total population (B03002_001) - Use the same state and year as before - Output format: wide - Challenge: You’ll need county codes, not names. Look at the GEOID patterns in your county data for hints.

# Define your race/ethnicity variables with descriptive names

# Use get_acs() to retrieve tract-level data
# Hint: You may need to specify county codes in the county parameter

# Calculate percentage of each group using mutate()
# Create percentages for white, Black, and Hispanic populations

# Add readable tract and county name columns using str_extract() or similar

TL <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = c(
    white_alone = "B03002_003",
    Black_African_American = "B03002_004",
    Hispanic_Latino = "B03002_012",
    total_population = "B03002_001"

  state = "PA",
  year = 2022,
  output = "wide"  # Makes analysis easier
)

  summarise(
    average_demographics_Hispanic_Latino = mean(Hispanic_Latino_percentage, na.rm = TRUE),
    average_demographics_white = mean(white_percentage, na.rm = TRUE),
    average_demographics_black = mean(black_percentage, na.rm = TRUE))
  
View(PP )
head(PP)

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
4.1 MOE Analysis for Demographic Variables
Your Task: Examine margins of error for demographic variables to see if some communities have less reliable data.
Requirements: - Calculate MOE percentages for each demographic variable - Flag tracts where any demographic variable has MOE > 15% - Create summary statistics

# Calculate MOE percentages for white, Black, and Hispanic variables

# Hint: use the same formula as before (margin/estimate * 100)


PAGES <- TL %>%
  mutate(
    white_percentage = round((white_aloneE / total_populationE) * 100, 2),
    black_percentage = round((Black_African_AmericanE / total_populationE) * 100, 2),
    Hispanic_Latino_percentage = round((Hispanic_LatinoE / total_populationE) * 100, 2)
    )

View(PAGES)
head(PAGES)

# A tibble: 6 × 13
  GEOID       NAME              white_aloneE white_aloneM Black_African_Americ…¹
  <chr>       <chr>                    <dbl>        <dbl>                  <dbl>
1 42001030101 Census Tract 301…         2317          153                      2
2 42001030103 Census Tract 301…         2384          341                      0
3 42001030104 Census Tract 301…         3114          359                      0
4 42001030200 Census Tract 302…         4294          423                     16
5 42001030300 Census Tract 303…         3619          220                    132
6 42001030400 Census Tract 304…         5006          287                     23
# ℹ abbreviated name: ¹​Black_African_AmericanE
# ℹ 8 more variables: Black_African_AmericanM <dbl>, Hispanic_LatinoE <dbl>,
#   Hispanic_LatinoM <dbl>, total_populationE <dbl>, total_populationM <dbl>,
#   white_percentage <dbl>, black_percentage <dbl>,
#   Hispanic_Latino_percentage <dbl>

# Create a flag for tracts with high MOE on any demographic variable

flag_t <- arrange(PAGES, desc(total_populationM))
view(flag_t)


PPPP <- PAGES %>%
      mutate(
      # Create reliability categories
         reliability = case_when(
         total_populationM < 400 ~ "High Confidence",
         total_populationM >= 400 & total_populationM <= 1000 ~ "Moderate",
         total_populationM > 1000 ~ "Low Confidence"))

count(PPPP, reliability)

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
4.2 Pattern Analysis
Your Task: Investigate whether data quality problems are randomly distributed or concentrated in certain types of communities.

# Group tracts by whether they have high MOE issues

# Calculate average characteristics for each group:
# - population size, demographic percentages
# Use group_by() and summarize() to create this comparison
# Create a professional table showing the patterns

II <- PPPP %>%
  group_by(reliability)%>%
  summarise(
    Average_MOE = mean(total_populationM, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_population_size = mean(total_populationE, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = 'drop')

kable(II,
      col.names = c("reliability", "Average_MOE", "Avg_population_size"),
      caption = "table showing the patterns",
      format.args = list(big.mark = ","))

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
5.1 Analysis Integration and Professional Summary
Your Task: Write an executive summary that integrates findings from all four analyses.
Executive Summary Requirements: 1. Overall Pattern Identification: What are the systematic patterns across all your analyses? 2. Equity Assessment: Which communities face the greatest risk of algorithmic bias based on your findings? 3. Root Cause Analysis: What underlying factors drive both data quality issues and bias risk? 4. Strategic Recommendations: What should the Department implement to address these systematic issues?
Executive Summary:
[First of all we took P13_001N code in the year 20220 to get ACS data which shows the data of Median age of the total population of all 52 states of US and it shows that Puerto Rico has the highest median age of 45.2 while Utah has the lowest Median age of the total population that is 31.3. After Puerto Rico we have Maine and then New Hampshire in the table list.I have chosen Pennsylvania for this analysis among these states. I have chosen this state because I am new here in this state and love it. In the second Part 2 I did County-Level Resource Assessment. First of all Data Retrieval was done. I used get_acs() to retrieve county-level data for Pennsylvania state. I used Geography: county level Variables, median household income (B19013_001) and total population (B01003_001) in the year 2022 to get ACS data. The table shows data with these columns total_populationM, total_populationE, median_household_incomeM, median_household_incomeE. Means figures with margin of errors too. Then as per task I Clean the county names to remove state name and “County” and “Pennsylvania”. Results show that Sullivan County, Pennsylvania have highest MOE while Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania has lowest MOE. In Data Quality Assessment I Calculate margin of error percentages and create reliability categories by using this formula = Calculate MOE percentage: (margin of error / estimate) * 100. So in results High Confidence was seen in 57 counties while Moderate Confidence was seen in 10 counties. Then I check High Uncertainty Counties and Identifed the 5 counties with the highest MOE percentages by using formula top_5_cont <- head(MOE[order(-MOE$moe_percentage), ], 5). Then I selected specific columns as per requirment by using selected_data <- select(top_5_cont, NAME, median_household_incomeE, median_household_incomeM, moe_percentage, reliability) formula. Data shows that Forest County, Pennsylvania is on firt while Elk County, Pennsylvania is on fifth number as per MOE Then I made Counties with Highest Income Data Uncertainty table by using kable. In Part 3 we moved to Neighborhood-Level Analysis. We Selected 2 counties from our reliability analysis for detailed tract-level study i.e Forest County, Pennsylvania and Sullivan County, Pennsylvania by using formula selected_counties <- filter(MOE, moe_percentage > 8). and stored the selected counties in a variable called selected_counties as per requirement. Showed the county name, median income, MOE percentage, reliability category in table. Next we moved to Tract-Level Demographics by using Geography: tract level - Variables: white alone (B03002_003), Black/African American (B03002_004), Hispanic/Latino (B03002_012), total population (B03002_001). We took same state = “PA” and year = 2022. It gave population of all three races and then we calculated MOE percentage as white_percentage, black_percentage, Hispanic_Latino_percentage. then We moved to Demographic Analysis where we analyzed the demographic patterns in tracts of PA. we analyzed that “Census Tract 195.02; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania” is with the highest percentage of Hispanic/Latino residents. we used the arrange() and slice() to get the top tract. then we Calculated average demographics by county using group_by() and summarize() functions. “Census Tract 195.02; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania” is with highest average demographic. Then we showed average percentage for each racial/ethnic group. Then we made a table of it as per requirement. with table deading as Counties with Ethnicities/races. In part 4 we did Comprehensive Data Quality Evaluation and evaluated margins of error for demographic variables to see if some communities have less reliable data. we saw High Confidence in 1897 counties, Moderate in 1453 and Low Confidence in 96 counties. So in a nutshell few counties have low Confidence i.e 96. we Created summary statistics showing how many tracts have data quality issues. A new table which shows on 96 counties with low Confidence is created. In Pattern Analysis we checked whether data quality problems are randomly distributed or concentrated in certain types of communities. We calculated Calculate average characteristics for each group like High Confidence Average_MOE is 227.6663, Low Confidence Average_MOE is 1,247.5833 and Moderate Confidence Average_MOE is 575.8121. and then created a table with heading “table showing the patterns”]

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
6.3 Specific Recommendations
Your Task: Create a decision framework for algorithm implementation.

# Create a summary table using your county reliability data
# Include: county name, median income, MOE percentage, reliability category

# Add a new column with algorithm recommendations using case_when():
# - High Confidence: "Safe for algorithmic decisions"
# - Moderate Confidence: "Use with caution - monitor outcomes"  
# - Low Confidence: "Requires manual review or additional data"

# Format as a professional table with kable()


JJ <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = c(
    white_alone = "B03002_003",
    Black_African_American = "B03002_004",
    Hispanic_Latino = "B03002_012",
    total_population = "B03002_001",
    median_household_income = "B19013_001"
  ),
  state = "PA",
  year = 2022,
  output = "wide"  # Makes analysis easier
)
View (JJ)

  mutate(

Key Recommendations:
Your Task: My data tables are almost enough and self descriptive even though I am giving my recommendations here.

Counties suitable for immediate algorithmic implementation: [Census Tract 9809; Allegheny County; Pennsylvania and Census Tract 369.01; Philadelphia County; Pennsylvania etc. are counties with high confidence data and due to less MOE these are appropriate for immediate algorithmic implementation]
Counties requiring additional oversight: [Census Tract 9506.07; Pike County; Pennsylvania and Census Tract 4101.01; Delaware County; Pennsylvania are counties with moderate confidence data and nominal monitoring would be needed]
Counties needing alternative approaches: [counties with low confidence data are “Census Tract 118.07; Lancaster County; Pennsylvania” and “Census Tract 112; Northampton County; Pennsylvania” definitely manual review and additional surveys are required]

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
Submission Checklist
Before submitting your portfolio link on Canvas:

All code chunks run without errors
All “[Fill this in]” prompts have been completed
Tables are properly formatted and readable
Executive summary addresses all four required components
Portfolio navigation includes this assignment
Census API key is properly set
Document renders correctly to HTML

Remember: Submit your portfolio URL on Canvas, not the file itself. Your assignment should be accessible at https://umairbinsaad.github.io/lab_1/lab1_template%20(1).html

title: Lab 0: Getting Started with dplyr
text:
Welcome to your first lab! In this (not graded) assignment, you’ll practice the fundamental dplyr operations I overviewed in class using car sales data. This lab will help you get comfortable with:

Basic data exploration
Column selection and manipulation

Creating new variables
Filtering data
Grouping and summarizing

Instructions: Copy this template into your portfolio repository under a lab_0/ folder, then complete each section with your code and answers. You will write the code under the comment section in each chunk. Be sure to also copy the data folder into your lab_0 folder.

title: Lab 0: Getting Started with dplyr
text:
1.1 Data Structure Exploration
Explore the structure of your data and answer these questions:

# Use glimpse() to see the data structure
glimpse(car_data)

title: Lab 0: Getting Started with dplyr
text:
2.1 Selecting Columns
Practice selecting different combinations of columns:

# Select just Model and Mileage columns
selected_data <- car_df %>%
  select(Model, Mileage)
View(selected_data)


# Select Manufacturer, Price, and Fuel type



# Challenge: Select all columns EXCEPT Engine Size

selected_datadd <- car_df %>%
  select(-"Engine size")
View(selected_datadd)

title: Lab 0: Getting Started with dplyr
text:
2.2 Renaming Columns
Let’s fix a problematic column name:

# Rename 'Year of manufacture' to year

ss <- rename(car_data, YY = "Engine size" )
View(ss)

# Check that it worked
names(car_data)

[1] "Manufacturer"        "Model"               "Engine size"        
[4] "Fuel type"           "Year of manufacture" "Mileage"            
[7] "Price"              


Question: Why did we need backticks around Year of manufacture but not around year?

title: Lab 0: Getting Started with dplyr
text:
3.1 Calculate Car Age

# Create an 'age' column (2025 minus year of manufacture)

pp <- car_data%>%
      mutate(
        "minus year of manufacture" = "ww"
      )
View(pp)

title: Lab 0: Getting Started with dplyr
text:
3.2 Categorize Cars

# Create a price_category column where if price is < 15000, its is coded as budget, between 15000 and 30000 is midrange and greater than 30000 is mid-range (use case_when)

GG <- car_data%>%
    mutate( 
      price_category = case_when(
        Price < 15000 ~ "budget",
        Price >= 15000 & Price <= 30000  ~ "midrange",
         Price > 30000 ~ "Luxury"
        
        
      ))
      
View(GG)

colnames(GG)

[1] "Manufacturer"        "Model"               "Engine size"        
[4] "Fuel type"           "Year of manufacture" "Mileage"            
[7] "Price"               "price_category"     

# Check your categories select the new column and show it
